sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Wayne, presents with a non-healing wound on his right foot, which he has had for 2-3 weeks.
The wound is a 1x2 inch circular lesion on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the right fifth MTP joint, with yellow slough and minimal granulation tissue.
The patient reports no pain or discomfort, but mentions that the wound sticks to his sock when he removes it.
He has a history of neuropathy and has been wearing new shoes that caused a blister, which eventually developed into the current wound.
Past medical history: The patient has diabetes, with a recent HbA1c of 8.1%.
He has a history of using a podiatrist, but they moved and he was unable to find a replacement.
Review of systems: The patient denies any symptoms of fever, chills, drainage, or odor from the wound.
He reports no trauma to the foot.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: Temperature 98.1°F, heart rate 72, respirations 16, blood pressure 110/65.
Physical exam: The wound is a 1x2 inch circular lesion on the dorsal aspect of the lateral right foot, proximal to the right fifth MTP joint, with yellow slough and minimal granulation tissue.
There is no surrounding erythema or cellulitis, no fluid collection, and no necrosis.
There is no odor.
Vascular exam reveals palpable bilateral femoral and popliteal pulses, with no palpable DP or PT pulses, but Doppler signs are present.
Test Results: The patient's right foot X-ray shows no evidence of osteomyelitis.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a diabetic foot ulcer on the right foot, with a wound that has not healed despite previous treatment with antibiotics.
Problem list: Diabetic foot ulcer; suspected impaired wound healing; diabetes mellitus; [NOT MENTIONED]
Plan:
I prescribed collagenase ointment to be applied to the wound once daily, covered with a dry sterile dressing.
I performed a debridement in the office to remove dead tissue.
I ordered an Ankle Brachial Index (ABI) to assess blood supply to the foot.
I instructed the patient to wear a surgical shoe to reduce pressure on the wound.
I do not think further antibiotics are necessary at this time.
Follow-up: The patient will return in two weeks for follow-up.
If the ABI indicates poor blood supply, a vascular specialist may be referred.
